verdicts.rationale: link.php line 19: the sink is `echo esc_url( $link_url )` inside an href attribute — `esc_url()` is the CORRECT sanitizer for URL-context output and fully neutralises XSS. The taint tool flagged it because it expected esc_html/esc_attr, but esc_url is the appropriate WordPress escape function for href attributes. The output is inside `href="..."` (line 19 of link.php), so esc_url() strips any javascript: or data: schemes. No XSS is possible.

video.php line 25: `echo wp_oembed_get( $video_meta, $settings )` — wp_oembed_get() is a WordPress core function that retrieves an oEmbed response from an allowlisted set of providers registered via wp_oembed_add_provider(). The returned HTML is generated by WordPress's oEmbed class (WP_oEmbed::get_html()), which is trusted internal output. The raw URL in $video_meta reaches wp_oembed_get() only as a lookup key (to match a provider and fetch the embed HTML from that provider's API). An attacker who controls only the stored post meta value cannot inject arbitrary HTML because: (1) the URL must match a registered oEmbed provider pattern; (2) the actual iframe/markup is returned by the provider's API, not derived from the stored URL string. The phpcs:ignore comment is appropriate here. No exploitable XSS.

video.php line 32: `echo wp_video_shortcode( array_merge( array( 'src' => esc_url( $video_meta ) ), $settings ) )`. The URL is passed through esc_url() before being used as the 'src' attribute value, and wp_video_shortcode() is a WordPress core function that generates a standard HTML5 <video> element. The sink is a trusted WordPress wrapper function, and esc_url() strips dangerous schemes from the URL. No XSS vector exists.

audio.php line 14: `echo wp_oembed_get( $audio_meta )` — same analysis as xss-002. wp_oembed_get() fetches HTML from an allowlisted oEmbed provider's API based on the URL lookup; the raw stored URL is not injected into HTML. The result is trusted WordPress-generated markup. No XSS.

audio.php line 26: `echo wp_audio_shortcode( array_merge( array( 'src' => esc_url( $audio_meta ) ), $settings ) )` — same analysis as xss-003. The stored URL is sanitized with esc_url() before being passed to the trusted WordPress core wp_audio_shortcode() function. No XSS.

product-category-list/image.php line 15: `echo qi_addons_for_elementor_get_list_shortcode_item_image( $images_proportion, $taxonomy_image, ... )`. The `$taxonomy_image` value from get_term_meta('thumbnail_id') is an attachment post ID (an integer). Tracing the callee in inc/shortcodes/helper.php lines 19-43: the value is passed as `$attachment_id` and used in either `qi_addons_for_elementor_get_attachment_image()` or `qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_generate_thumbnail()`, both of which pass the ID through `intval()` before calling wp_get_attachment_image() or vt_resize(). An integer attachment ID cannot carry XSS payload. No exploitable XSS.

helper.php lines 246-277: get_post_meta value flows into qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_get_inline_style() -> qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_get_inline_attr(). Inspecting the callee (helper.php lines 336-388): before output, the value is processed at line 381 with `esc_attr( sanitize_text_field( html_entity_decode( $properties ) ) )` and then at line 382 with `preg_replace( '/<[^>]*>|#x3[ce];|<|>/i', '', $sanitized_properties )`. The attribute key is also stripped to `[-_a-z0-9]+` chars only. Additionally, line 375 blocks any value starting with 'on' (event handlers) or containing 'href'. While the generated string is echoed without a wrapping esc_attr at the call site, the inner function already applies esc_attr internally. The taint analyzer missed the sanitization inside the called function. No exploitable XSS.

helper.php lines 246-306: get_post_meta value flows into qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_get_class_attribute() -> qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_get_inline_attr(). Identical sanitization chain as xss-007: esc_attr(), sanitize_text_field(), html_entity_decode(), plus preg_replace stripping tags and entity bypasses applied internally before output. The attribute name ('class') cannot be controlled. No exploitable XSS.

helper.php lines 246-401: get_post_meta value flows into qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_get_inline_attr() directly. Same sanitization chain as xss-007/008 applied inside qi_addons_for_elementor_framework_get_inline_attr(): esc_attr() + sanitize_text_field() + html_entity_decode() + regex strip on line 381-382, plus 'on' event handler and 'href' blocking on line 375. The taint analyzer missed the sanitization inside the callee. No exploitable XSS.

class-qiaddonsforelementor-admin-notice.php lines 252-279: handle_deactivation() is an authenticated wp_ajax_ handler (no nopriv_ variant) that first calls check_ajax_referer() on line 253. The $request_handler_url is a hardcoded string literal 'https://api.qodeinteractive.com/plugin-deactivation-feedback.php' on line 263 — it is NOT derived from any user input. The wp_remote_post() URL cannot be redirected or controlled by an attacker. The taint tool incorrectly traced sanitize_text_field($_POST[...]) into the $data array body and then flagged the wp_remote_post() call as SSRF, but $data is only the POST body sent to the hardcoded URL, not the URL itself. The $_POST values in $data are sanitized with sanitize_text_field() + wp_unslash(). This is an intentional plugin deactivation feedback telemetry feature, not SSRF.

summary.overall_assessment: All 10 flagged findings are false positives. The 9 XSS taint flows fail for these reasons: (1) esc_url() is the correct sanitizer for href-context output (xss-001); (2) wp_oembed_get(), wp_video_shortcode(), and wp_audio_shortcode() are trusted WordPress core functions — the user-supplied URL is a provider lookup key, not injected HTML (xss-002 through xss-005); (3) the taxonomy thumbnail_id is an integer attachment ID that passes through intval() before use as wp_get_attachment_image() argument (xss-006); (4) the inline style/class/attr helper functions apply esc_attr() + sanitize_text_field() + regex stripping internally, which the taint tool missed because it doesn't trace into callees (xss-007 through xss-009). The single SSRF finding is a false positive: the request URL is a hardcoded string literal, the user-controlled data is only the POST body (sanitized with sanitize_text_field()), and the handler requires a valid nonce and admin authentication. Qi Addons for Elementor appears to implement appropriate sanitization throughout, though in several cases the phpcs:ignore comments suggest the developers are aware of (and comfortable with) the taint-tracking limitations.
